video_title: Did The Bust Just Begin? | Jesse Felder
gist: Jesse Felder argues the AI capex boom is already metastasizing into a bust as data-center delays turn into cancellations, forcing semiconductor analyst estimates down, while a simultaneous stagflationary macro setup (oil toward $150, 10-yr toward 6%, Fed forced to tighten) makes him bearish on a broad market over 50% entangled in the AI trade and bullish on energy/commodities and diversification.

themes.theme: AI buildout physics & the coming bust

insights.claim: The AI boom is shifting into a bust right now, with the first data-center cancellations (after a year of delays) being the writing on the wall that will force analysts to reprice semiconductors.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Watch for accelerating data-center cancellations and downward semiconductor estimate revisions as the trigger for a tech-led selloff.
insights.quote: Now we're starting to see the first cancellations. To me, that's that this is the writing on the wall.

insights.claim: Roughly two-thirds of data centers slated for completion next year haven't even broken ground, making projected semiconductor revenue physically impossible to absorb.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Forward semiconductor revenue estimates rest on capacity that cannot exist on schedule; chip demand estimates must fall.
insights.quote: there's been statistics that show twothirds of uh all the data centers that are supposed to be completed next year haven't even be broken ground yet

insights.claim: The reported AI earnings boom is a timing illusion: hyperscalers recognize chip/buildout revenue immediately while deferring depreciation expenses, overstating near-term profitability.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: As depreciation from ~$750B of capex ramps, hyperscaler net income could fall sharply even if revenue holds.
insights.quote: revenues are recognized immediately and expenses are recognized, you know, later on

insights.claim: About three-quarters of a trillion dollars is being spent on the AI buildout this year, but the chips have nowhere to go because the data centers to house them aren't ready.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Inventory is piling up at middlemen like Super Micro, signaling a glut that precedes price collapses.

insights.claim: Felder would only abandon his bearish AI call if the physics of building multi-gigawatt data centers miraculously sped up; he cites Satya Nadella saying Microsoft has chips but no 'warm shells' to plug them into.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: The build-out timeline, not chip supply, is the binding constraint to monitor for any bull-case reversal.
insights.quote: we can buy all the chips that we want. We don't have the warm shells to plug them into

insights.claim: A large data-center operator put a 1-1.5 gigawatt project on hold this week at the customer's request (speculated to be Microsoft), signaling demand-driven scaling back, not just construction delays.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Customer-initiated holds (vs. supply delays) are the bust signal; track hyperscaler capex guidance for cancellations.

insights.claim: Felder draws a direct analogy to the railroad boom ending the moment redundant parallel routes were cancelled; AI is at that same inflection as the first cancellations appear.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Historical capex-bubble pattern: first cancellation marks the boom-to-bust pivot; treat current cancellations as that marker.

insights.claim: Semiconductor stocks trade at the highest valuations in history with price-to-sales ratios off the chart, leaving extreme downside if estimates roll over, and the sector fell 10% on Friday.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Valuation extremity amplifies the repricing risk; the sector could fall 40-50%+ in a bust scenario.
insights.quote: Semiconductor stocks are trading at the highest valuations in history. Price to sales ratio is just off the chart for the sector.

insights.claim: For the second or third week this year, more analyst price targets were reduced than increased, an early sign analysts are starting to capitulate on the AI trade.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: A turn in the breadth of estimate revisions is a leading indicator that the repricing has begun.

insights.claim: Demand projections were inflated during a 'token-maxing' period when AI was subsidized to roughly one-tenth of true cost by VC-funded OpenAI/Anthropic; the shift to per-token pricing is already cutting demand.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: True-cost token pricing rations agentic usage, so compute demand forecasts underpinning data-center buildouts are too high.
insights.quote: We're only ultimately I think at onetenth or less of the true cost of AI.

insights.claim: AI inference is migrating onto local devices, evidenced by strong Apple Mac mini sales and Dell/Nvidia developing on-device AI chips, structurally reducing the need for data-center compute.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: On-device AI is a secular headwind to cloud data-center demand; watch device-AI adoption as a leading indicator.

insights.claim: Sam Altman's strategic incentive is for the cost of compute to crash so OpenAI can run a profitable business, which Felder sees as undermining the case for ever-higher chip/data-center spend.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: If the key buyer wants compute cheap, sustained high semiconductor pricing power is unlikely.
insights.quote: It's in his best interest for there to be a crash in the cost of compute

insights.claim: Companies tout token-request growth as a valuation metric, creating an incentive to spin up bots that inflate token requests, raising the risk of usage being self-dealt or fraudulent.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Token-request growth may be a low-quality KPI; discount AI valuations built on it.

insights.claim: Amazon and Google employees are reportedly mocking the value of mandated AI models internally, which Felder compares to Henry Blodget's dot-com practice of publicly rating stocks while privately calling them trash.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Insider skepticism about ROI is a contrarian tell that AI productivity claims are overstated.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers have left their separate monopoly sandboxes (Google/search, Meta/social, Microsoft/software) to compete head-on in one capital-intensive AI arena, collapsing free cash flow to zero or negative.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Margin and FCF erosion from mutual competition threatens the 'Magnificent Seven' premium; Felder frames a 'Maleficent 7' downgrade.

insights.claim: Hyperscalers are issuing large amounts of bonds to fund AI spending precisely because free cash flow shows they aren't generating the cash to pay for it.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Rising debt issuance to fund capex is a red flag on profitability; watch FCF and bond supply from mega-caps.
insights.quote: they have to go sell so many bonds to raise money because they don't have the cash flow to pay for a lot of this spending

insights.claim: Citing Jeremy Grantham, the next decade for hyperscalers will look so unlike the last as to be shocking, with cash-generating monopolies giving way to dubious-profitability competition.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: The 'second wave' of the bust is a re-rating of mega-cap quality itself, not just chips.
insights.quote: the next decade for the hyperscalers is going to look so unlike the last decade as to be shocking

insights.claim: The latest NFIB small-business survey shows firms raising prices while sharply cutting hiring plans, a leading indicator of simultaneous rising unemployment and inflation (stagflation).
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Stagflation signal undercuts both the soft-landing narrative and Fed rate-cut hopes.

insights.claim: Oil-market fundamentals justify a price near $150/barrel; current prices are artificially suppressed by China importing ~5M bpd less than normal and record-fast strategic reserve drawdowns.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Suppression mechanisms are temporary; an oil breakout toward $150 is the core macro catalyst to position for.
insights.quote: the fundamentals of the the energy um sector of the, you know, crude oil suggest crude oil should probably be trading $150 a barrel right now

insights.claim: Gold's two-year surge is a leading indicator (per Tom McClellan's work) that historically precedes a ~20-month rise in broad commodities, oil, and interest rates, now just starting to feed through.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Gold's run implies oil to $150 and the 10-yr yield potentially to 6% over 6-12 months; position for higher rates and commodities.
insights.quote: Gold is a really good leading indicator for the ro the broader commodity space

insights.claim: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopened today, experts say it would take six to eight months to restore 80% of pre-war oil flow, supporting a sustained oil price breakout.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Supply recovery lag means an oil shock persists even on de-escalation; bullish for energy regardless of ceasefire headlines.

insights.claim: A commodity-led inflation surge (CPI ~4%, core PCE running hotter) will force the Fed to raise rates and pull the punch bowl, ending the bubble much as past Fed tightening cycles have.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Markets pricing rate cuts/energy normalization are positioned opposite Felder's view; rising oil is what forces the Fed's hand.

insights.claim: More than 50% of stocks are now effectively caught up in the AI trade (including names like Ford and Caterpillar), making it nearly impossible to avoid broad equity pain in an AI bust.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Diversification within US equities offers little protection; index and ETF exposure carries hidden AI concentration.

insights.claim: The economy is far more dependent on the AI wealth effect and direct capex injection than during the dot-com bust, so an AI selloff would hit the real economy harder than 2001-2003.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Unlike 2000, an AI bust likely drags GDP and the consumer down, raising recession and bear-market odds.

insights.claim: The consumer is already strained, with real income falling for consecutive months as inflation outpaces wages and credit-card defaults at multi-year highs, while housing is on life support.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: A weak consumer base means any AI-driven downturn lands on an already-fragile economy.

insights.claim: Market structure is extraordinarily speculative, with heavy call-option buying and leveraged ETFs; Buffett called it more casino-like than ever and Berkshire holds $300B+ cash, its most defensive posture in company history.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Buffett's record cash hoard is a behavioral signal to be fearful while others are greedy.
insights.quote: There's a reason why Bergkshire Hathaway has 300 plus billion dollars in cash

insights.claim: Consumer sentiment has fallen to the lowest reading in the index's history (worse than the GFC or 9/11), yet households remain badly under-positioned for a recession and bear market.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Record-low sentiment plus high equity allocations means a downturn would be a severe shock to unprepared households.

insights.claim: Energy is the top hedge against an AI collapse: at ~3% of the S&P 500 versus a typical 8-12%, with cheap stocks not pricing in the ~50% YTD oil rally, and it trades contrary to tech.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Underweight, contrarian, fundamentally cheap energy is the favored AI-bust hedge and multi-year overweight.
insights.quote: the energy sector is 3% of the S&P 500 and it's and it's typically been closer to, you know, 8 10 12%

insights.claim: Real assets and commodities can win in both an AI boom and bust, because the physical buildout (power, electrical infrastructure, energy) must happen regardless while the bust mainly hits AI valuations.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Commodity producers offer asymmetric exposure with downside protection relative to AI equities.

insights.claim: Felder applies the capital-cycle framework: capital over-flooded tech into oversupply while starving real assets, setting up a commodity super-cycle as capital eventually rotates back.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Capital-cycle logic supports a multi-year commodity overweight as the mirror image of the tech glut.

insights.claim: Felder expects precious metals to stay under pressure near-term (silver's outperformance of gold and surging gold-fund inflows signal froth) and favors energy over precious metals for now.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Rotate within commodities toward energy; wait for gold-ETF outflows and the end of Fed tightening to re-enter precious metals.

insights.claim: The best precious-metals buying opportunity will come when the Fed's tightening phase ends and capitulation hits (people dumping gold ETFs) ahead of a likely pivot back to cutting.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Watch for gold-ETF outflows and signs the Fed has done enough as the entry signal for precious metals.

insights.claim: Felder calls for a 'new bull market in diversification' after 15 years of US-tech concentration: own international stocks, real assets, fixed income, and cash, plus contrarian defensive sectors like healthcare and consumer staples showing insider buying.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Rotate away from concentrated US tech toward broad diversification and out-of-favor defensives with insider buying.
insights.quote: we're probably at the cusp of a new bull market in diversification
